item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found released under Creative Commons. Thus, it is to be assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a fan, unfortunately, and it's a downvote from me. Unlike some people, I don't particularly mind 'convenient log at the end', because if there's one thing I've learned about this modern world it's that people LOVE to document, but this log is just so over the top that it fails to convey any emotion other than disdain.

I get that it's supposed to be a guy in a breakdown but someone that's already broken down doesn't have any connection to me. The key element of horror is the breaking down, not the results. It's dragging the reader into the steady escalation and carrying them along to the disturbing climax. If Freddy Krueger just leaps out and cleaves the protagonist in half, it's not horror, it's a pop scare. This is the literary equivalent.

I'm also not fond of "the Foundation can relatively easily erase the existence of 30,000 random people without significant difficulty." Which maybe it wasn't intended to seem that simple, but given that it's not gone into in any depth, it sure seems that way. Even for an organization with the resources of the Foundation, a cover-up of that scale should be a momentous challenge, not 'all in a day's work.' I'm not sure that I'm necessarily speaking for anyone else on this, though, so take it for what you think it's worth.

item.title: Major article changes
item.content:encoded:
I've added some major changes to the article. The containment procedures now outline actions to be taken if the site disappears again, and I have included task force Gamma 5 in the section about information about the park after it disappeared.
Hope these changes really improve the immersion of the story and the article itself :)

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Much better, thanks!

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: I have compressed the images so now they total around 659 KB, hope this helps. 👍

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I did, thanks so much for pointing this out! Never noticed this typo before, somehow

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Author, the images you have here total 5.11 MB, which is murder on mobile devices with limited data plans. Any chance you can resize those? If you're unsure of how to do so, I can help fix the image sizes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this SCP overall, but just wanted to confirm a detail quickly. When you mentioned "Stanfordshire", did you perhaps mean "Staffordshire"?

item.title: This was waaaayyy better than mine.
item.content:encoded:
Hey scarredparadise, I had this slot before my SCP was deleted,I like what you've done with the place.

:-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not opposed to anomalous events being used as concepts, but this just doesn't work as an article, especially when the only evidence of anomalous activity was eyewitnesses and a notebook.

Also agreeing that the note itself is melodramatic, and gives no interesting details about just what happened to the park itself. It's a cut-down version of SCP-1733 that needs serious reworking.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: No problem. Thank you :)

item.title: Staff Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Author, I have cleared the tags from this page as only a few of them are actual tags in our system. If you're unfamiliar with our tagging system, please do not tag your own article. A member of our tagging staff will tag it within a few days. Please be patient.

item.title: Thanks for the feedback
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all so much for all your feedback! It means a lot that people have taken the time to read the article and give very constructive feedback to help me improve!

I have made changes to the entire article based on your feedback and I hope you all enjoy the new and improved SCP-3157.

Again, thank you all so much for commenting and helping me make a half-decent SCP. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ptagtoray pugatry blood purgtory darkness etenal

If there had been some kind real buildup, this might have been alright. As it stands, it's excessively melodramatic, and I can't take it even remotely seriously. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I missing something, or did people decide to start committing mass suicide after only a few months trapped in an amusement park? Killing their children while they're at it? That doesn't seem to be typical human behavior, unless I'm missing something before then. It would make more sense to me for that to be after the park's rides start malfunctioning and killing people. Is there a reason for the suicides beforehand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, this would probably hit me better if I hadn't been to Alton Towers myself many times (and side note, It's strange how small it looks from above.)

Seconding other people here, the tone is what compromises it: the except is okay, but the foundation written piece is lacking a clinical, detail-orientated tone. While it's great to be descriptive, it also has to be descriptive in a way that would make sense in the setting. The utilities were 'ripped' out, instead perhaps say that they were severed (and was it a clean cut, as if something sliced through them? were they yanked about and damaged like they were physically torn? And when the stolen volume is returned, is any damage to the utilities repaired?) Was that shockwave a visual effect, or was it a thunderclap as if air had suddenly rushed into a sudden vacuum left behind by the vanishing. These are not actually central to the overall mood of the piece but contribute both to that clinical vibe and how the reader visualizes the event in question.

Stuff like that, providing clear, concise detail, is what can make or break a piece imitating clinical tone.

As a side note, born from the fact that i've been there, I'm not entirely sure why you've changed the names of the theme park and the Oblivion ride in particular— though I suppose i can understand the purgatory theme. The piece would have worked better on me, personally, if you had instead stuck with the actual place, and built the anomaly around that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I like the premise behind this, and a lot of the elements you're going with; I think the main things preventing me from upvoting are largely issues of tone, grammar, and presentation:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-3157 is the former location of the Holmswood Towers Amusement Park resort following the anomalous events 3157-A and 3157-B, the sudden disappearance and reappearance of the entire resort.

This is a parenthetical statement; as such, I suspect it would work better in parenthesis:

SCP-3157 is the former location of the Holmswood Towers Amusement Park resort following the anomalous events 3157-A and 3157-B (the disappearance and reappearance of the resort, respectively).

I took out 'sudden' and 'entire' because they're both a bit unclinical and also very unnecessary.

On the 16th of May 2016, at approximately 10:32 AM, the entire site disappeared, including all individuals present on the site at the time of disappearance, which has since been labelled Event-3157-A.

This sentence has way too many clauses. You could clean it up quite a bit:

At 10:32 am on the 16th of May 2016, SCP-3157 and all individuals present on-site disappeared. This has been designated Event-3157-A.

I took out 'approximately' because you're not being approximate — you're being precise (10:32). Also, 'designated' is more formal than 'labelled', I feel.

This is an ongoing problem throughout the piece — you use a lot of extra clauses and extra words. A lot of your prose is just restating things already established, or emphasizing things that are already clear.

There's also another nitpick I have regarding information you supply the reader in the notebook:

We coudln’t starve to death, the whole park reset after each day so we had a permanent supply.

This feels very much like the author inserting their hand into the narrative to clarify something to the reader (why they didn't starve). I feel this would work much better if it just said that the whole park reset after each day (thus allowing the reader to figure out how they survived/didn't starve).


Let me emphasize that I actually like this piece; typically, I don't enjoy anomalies that are essentially just notebooks written by someone in the aftermath, but in this case, I think it worked well. The only things that are stopping my upvote here is the language itself, which feels too clunky and awkward in several places. With a bit more polish, it'd be an easy +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with all cables, water pipes and phone lines left ripped out, torn from their appliances as if the entire site itself had been ripped from the ground.

Really poor tone here. Everything after the comma should go, and I'd use "exposed" rather than "ripped out" in the first part.

Why would they demolish the amusement park?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's embarrassing 😕
Thanks for pointing it out 👍

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short, but does an excellent job of creeping me out. Easy +1.

Minor quibble: You misspelled "Purgatory" in footnote 2.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: The edit has been reverted, and the user is now banned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-3157 was edited by Hero_of_ISIS. Mods, excuse me, but could do something about this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to get off SCP-3157's wild ride :(
